sentences.text: Was-- after the Russian took over, it was closed.
No continuation.
This was the end.
It was cooperatives-- government.
And my father started to work in a cooperative-- a government cooperative.
And they make him foreman, and he was a foreman over there.
And we make a [? poor ?] living, I will say.
My mother never worked.
Housekeeper, like used to be.
My mother have a hobby.
She used to like read-- to read lots of literature.
And like, you know, it used to be in this time, the woman used to spend all day by cooking, to prepare the dinner and supper for the kids, and washing and cleaning.
And she was always busy with her own work.
With five children.
And in free time she used to have, she used to like to read books-- literature-- and listen to the radio.
No, it was-- was a little bit changes, regarding traditional.
After the Russian came in, we feel already slowed down.
We didn't attend already the synagogue every Saturday.
And it was slowed down a little bit.
After the war started.
From 1939 to 1941.
I attend the school to learn Russian language in school.
And [? like ?] to prepare myself for the Russian school.
I missed a couple months.
The time has gone quickly.
And when I already decided, the parent decided to put me back, to continue my education with the Russian didn't build up right away overnight the schools.
I changed not only the school, the whole system.
It was already too late.
The war started.
I finished only sixth grade, plus cheder, when the war was started.
We didn't feel so much when the war started.
We saw the Ukraine populations preparing to celebrate something.
We feel this.
Everybody dressed nice, clean-- the Ukrainian people.
We were surrounded with lots of Ukrainian people.
And every-- each sharpening the teeth.
No, we didn't feel that right away.
Now, people were scared, and they evacuated, and they left the town.
And just wealthy people, they had-- there was-- they have opinion-- they used to have opinion the German will not touch them [INAUDIBLE] [? the ?] rich.
And the German is killing only the workers, the poor, and the Communist.
And this kept them-- not all of them-- partly-- to stay and not to leave his own.
And some of them was really difficult to make a decision, extreme decision.
In a normal life, in his own home, and to leave it.
And to go-- they didn't know where to go.
And to grab something what they can only carry in the two hand and go by feet.
It was not everybody what could make the decision.
So I would say, in my town, half of the people didn't left.
They-- Stayed.
Yes.
But half of the people--
They killed them.
Half of the people left-- evacuated.
Pardon me?
They gone to deep Russia-- gone to the station.
The-- some of them took off in trains, and some of them by feet, and some of them rent horses.
And what way [INAUDIBLE] they gone different way.
To back, back, back, deep to Russia.
To save their life.
Pardon me?
I'm talking-- yeah, the only Jewish people.
No.
No.
We usually-- even family didn't know.
It was so much mixed up.
Was lost-- the husband lost the wife, and the wife lost her husband.
And some time, the sirens is alarming to-- to run away when the German planes came to bomb the train.
In these kind of panics people lost each the other.
They lost the children.
Some lost the husband, others the wife.
Nobody know what direction-- We-- after the war, we find out.
Some of my landsmen was in Siberia, some of my landsmen was in [INAUDIBLE].
We just after the war, we find out.
Oh, there was bombing every day.
Pardon me?
For us, it was new, absolutely.
They was flying and bombing, and they-- they was-- the-- the planes prepared the road for the army to come in.
They bombed-- they-- they attacked the population.
They scared them.
And after the-- the planes bombing, the army start to-- to go-- to come in.
You see, what-- my parents left.
I refused to leave it.
I was attached to my grandfather and grandmother.
And I was-- I have a big, deep feeling, and I was worried who will take care for them.
And I told my parents, I'm going to stay with the grandfather.
Everybody left, and I was-- I was left with my grandfather and grandmother.
My parents left in-- June the 22nd, 2:00 in the night, it-- the war start.
My parents left in June the 28th, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the town.
I personally didn't know.
Nobody know where he's going.
They left, and that's all.
To run away from the city, and to go with the Russian army.
With the Russian army is going to go with them, deep to Russia, just for protection.
They didn't know where they can find a protection.
People left by instinct.
They gone by instinct.
So your parents followed the Russian army.
Yeah-- the Russian army stepped out, and the people stepped out with them.
Under their protection.
Pardon me?
The children.
My father was-- they took him in the army.
And he was in the army, which he-- later, I found out he became ill, [? time they ?] let him out.
He died from typhus, my father.
He died in 19-- 1942 in Asia.
He was over there-- Respublik Russian, in Tashkent.
Yes.
I'll tell you the truth-- was not time even to manage.
When my-- my father, they took him in the army, after eight days, I think-- six days-- they left already.
They left the town.
No, my father, they took him in the army, and my mother took the kids, and she was gone with the kids.
She went-- followed the Russian army.
Absolutely, yeah.
Which-- nobody know where is going.
But no one know.
Nobody know.
Some people planned to go to Kiev.
And when they came close to Kiev, Kiev was bombed very heavy.
They missed the city, and they gone already the different direction.
Which-- nobody-- nobody could predict it, exactly, in what direction you were going.
So you stayed on with your grandparents.
Yes.
Then people start to come in my-- in our town, from Rowne.
Eyewitnesses, which they saw it-- their closest, the family, was killed by the German army.
Not only by the German army but the local Ukrainian people.
They knived them with the hacks-- they hacked them.
They chopped them with the axe, [? the axe. ?] And the people came to give the message to us in the city.
Then my grandmother forced me to take my bicycle and to leave home.
This was-- this was in June-- in June 29-- June 30.
End of June.
No, 13 and a half.
13 and a half.
Where did she suggest that you go?
I didn't know where to go.
I never left home.
I took my bicycle.
My grandmother gave me a blanket.
She gave me a bread.
And she gave me a little bit butter, a little bit-- to-- to the bread.
And I kissed them goodbye, and I gone by instinct.
I asked people, where's the road to go to Russia?
And the people show me the road to go to Russia.
And this is the hand which I tell goodbye my parents-- my grandmother and grandfather, and I left.
And I left-- I couldn't go with the highways.
Bombed-- they was bombed, the highways.
Then some peoples gone-- we-- we have lots of forests over there.
Then I was gone in forest.
And then I find out already was surrounded, with the German army, around, which some people find a way to go out.
And we was out from this surrounding, from the German army in the forest.
And I was-- I slept.
I was tired.
And when I woke up in the morning, my bicycle is gone, my blanket is gone, and some food I have which I find in the road-- some potatoes, a couple greens.
And everything disappeared, and I left like this-- hungry, tired, my clothes already damaged from the forest, which I was sleeping, over there.
And then I didn't have another choice, like, to go together with the army.
I was in the forest-- it was, I would say, eight, nine days, I was sleeping and-- and running in the forest.
You see, in forest, always you can find wild apple.
And summertime is not problem, something [? to. ?] You-- I used to go out to our garden, when I used to see our garden.
I used to go out at nighttime, something to pick up from garden.
Cucumber or something.
Yeah, no, you see, everybody was of his own.
And when people was already-- like, two brothers or two sisters, with their mother, then there was a way-- you-- you didn't know where's the luck direction-- or where is the luck.
You never know.
Some people gone to the left, and some people gone this direction.
Some people figure out that they can save their life-- they have a little bit of gold or money, and they can save their lives.
Some farmers will help them to save their life, which-- they took away the money from them, and they give them over to the German.
No, the feeling what the people have, their instinct, was just false, what the people had.
Yes.
And this is-- I was alone.
I would say five days I was alone in forest.
No.
What I said-- then I was already exhausted.
And I came out from forest to the highway.
Then I saw it-- army is stepping out-- some is injured-- with wagons.
And I was with them together.
Russian.
No.
Pardon me.
Not-- after this time, I was with them together.
And we came to Korosten with the army.
Korosten-- this is a small town in Ukraine, which was a respublik from Russia.
And when we came in Korosten, they accept me like a son in the army.
And they have lots of children in the Russian army.
Which they used them, sometime, to-- to give them information from the German army.
But the German didn't give attention for young kids.
And the young kids could go and to sneak out.
And it was lots of children, when they lost their parents, was in the army.
And I'm one from them.
So you attached yourself to the Russian army for support.
Yes.
They give me this army pair of pants, which they didn't have pants for children, and I was small.
And big pants, a big shirt-- big boots.
When was raining, my boot always left behind.
My feet came out from the boot-- very big.
And I was in the army.
And then, in the nighttime, they gave me a gun-- which, already, they learn me the way to use a gun.
And I was in patrol.
I was in nighttime.
I was watching a building over there.
And I slept.
And somebody stole my gun.
And when they stole my gun, the military tribunal took me-- they took me away.
And they put me in a quarantine-- like a jail, in a basement.
And then this start already my problem, which I didn't understand the meaning, what is the tribunal.
The military tribunal in time from war.
And I didn't understand, very serious is, in time from war, to lose a gun.
And they put me in basement.
And I think is looking like a Jewish investigator in the military tribunal.
And he came in to interview me.
And I was crying-- a young kid.
And I told him-- they figure up I sold that or I changed for food.
And I told him just what happened.
I slept, and--
And you're not supposed to sleep when you're a soldier.
No, what kind soldier I was?
I was a young child.
And I told him what happened.
He said, the tribunal don't believe in tears.
And he was looking out the window, and he said, I-- I can't tell you what to do.
Do what you want.
Then I figured up-- aha, he was looking out the window, and it was raining-- very bad weather.
And so, with a little box, I putted up the box.
I was a small, little child.
And I stepped up.
And I open the window with a bar, and I run away.
I was running I didn't know how many miles in an hour.
It was raining.
I was wet-- tired.
And I fell asleep under some building-- I don't even--
This is between-- between Kharkov and Stalingrad, in Russia.
No, between-- between-- yeah, between Kharkov and Stalingrad.
And I fell asleep.
And after I woke up myself, I was walking, and I find a little bag.
Which in this bag I find food, I finded, and some IDs.
IDs.
And a birth certificate The name was Felix Sempatia.
Sempatia.
Felix-- F-E-L-X-- F-E-L-I-X.
And Semtiya-- S-E-M-T-I-Y-A.
And came to myself an idea.
Dimerman, they're going to search.
I lost a gun.
I-- I never was-- I never was involved with-- in criminal offenses.
I didn't understand would be arrested.
I was a young child.
I was very, very scared.
And I figure up they can shoot me, [? if ?] something happen.
And the mind never-- never left me.
I was in jail, in my own body!
And I figure up only this can help me-- to change my identity.
And, with these [? came ?] papers, I came up to another army.
And the army took me in.
And I was with this army, and I was accepted again, in another army.
Luck-- 'Felix'-- Semtiya, French.
And some way in time from war, I find a French dictionary, and I start to learn a couple French words.
Which is-- you can be Jewish and not to speak Jewish.
This is normally.
Some people is assimilated.
This is paper-- the Frenchman is born in in Poland-- in Gdynia, Gdansk.
Yeah, deep Poland, yeah.
I learned the certificate was one year older like I was.
And not to forget what you're born-- the name.
And I was in the army up to the end, under this name.
Yes.
This is the Third Ukrainian Front.
The Third Ukrainian Front.
What We we this the Third Ukrainian Front.
Ukraine, like I told you-- this is a respublik of Russia.
And they have-- was fighting millions of people.
And the army have so many people in a division, and so many divisions can go in each front.
There was First Ukrainian front, Second Ukrainian Front, Third Ukrainian Front, Fourth Ukrainian Front.
I was in the Third Ukrainian Front-- this-- this main division.
We belonged to this kind type of division.
This was the Third Ukrainian Front.
Everybody was fighting against the German.
Everybody was-- this is the Russian army.
This is all the Russian army.
Under one commander, from Moscow.
And the division was divided.
Oh, with what-- I was in the Belarusian army-- Belarusian front.
Second Belarusian Front.
Pardon me?
No, no, this was Belarusian front, already.
This was an army, but they designed to fight Belarusia, a different [INAUDIBLE] respublik.
To make free this [INAUDIBLE] respublik from the German occupation.
And this army was designed to make free from Ukrainian the territory.
From Ukrainian.
I was a [? child. ?] I used to wash the-- the pails from the kitchen.
I used to wash-- to help them.
I used to help them to wash the kitchen, to bring water, and to take the horses-- to wash the horses-- and to peel potatoes for a period of time.
I was a [? child. ?] And we used to always scrub cattles with us.
And in daytime, we used to take cattles to go away a little bit far-- a couple miles away.
And was dressed like a little shepherd.
And we used to watch where's the German army and what kind of movement-- what is going on-- and to bring the messages.
Usually we was treated like adult.
We feel-- they give us to grow, in the army.
Everybody was-- was ready of his own to protect himself, to defend himself.
I was treated like adult.
We wouldn't-- was treat-- there was educated-- they tried to educate us not to look for protection.
To be alone--
Self-- yeah.
